The Bank of Japan held its monetary policy settings unchanged today, hours after the Federal Reserve ended 2024 with a third-straight interest-rate cut.

item.title: US Vietnam Policy Unlikely to Change With Trump, US Admiral Says
item.description:
• A continuity of Indo-Pacific policies has existed, he says
  • US attack, transport aircraft displayed at Hanoi defense expo

The US will most likely continue its current policy of engaging Vietnam in military operations and activities during the incoming Trump administration, the head of US Indo-Pacific Command said at a briefing in Hanoi.

“There has been very little difference from a policy standpoint, from the previous administration to the present administration, on the focus of a free and independent and open Indo-Pacific,” US Admiral Samuel Paparo said at the Hanoi defense expo, referring to the first administration of Donald Trump and the current Biden White House. “I should think that we should continue on with policies, and particularly with military operations and activities, that should demonstrate continuity within the relationship.”

The US sent A-10 Thunderbolt II attack aircraft and a C-130J Super Hercules military transport plane to be on display at Vietnam’s four-day defense expo with representatives from 49 countries.

“We seek to have our great defense companies working here, working with Vietnam, with Vietnamese counterparts on things potentially like joint production” and technology transfers, US Ambassador to Vietnam Marc Knapper said.

Vietnam aims to promote “cooperation and development” in foreign relations and defense to build trust for regional peace and stability, Prime Minister Pham Minh Chinh said at the event’s opening. Vietnam seeks to forge foreign policy of self-reliance and diversification of relationships, he said.

The Southeast Asian country has a long-standing territorial dispute in the South China Sea with China, which claims the majority of the area as its territory — an assertion that overlaps with separate claims by Brunei, Malaysia and Taiwan as well as the Philippines.

Read: Vietnam, China Hold ‘Frank’ Talks After Alleged Sea Attack

Vietnam aims to have a modern military “to build defense potential strong enough to enhance self-defense and protect the country,” Chinh said.

The US and Vietnam have been gradually expanding defense cooperation since the two nations upgraded bilateral ties to a comprehensive strategic partnership last year during the visit of President Joe Biden . Biden and Vietnam’s former party chief Nguyen Phu Trong pledged to further cooperate in defense industry and trade, according to a joint statement of the two leaders. They also agreed to boost dialog for cooperation in maritime law enforcement, maritime security capacity, cybercrime and high-tech crime.

The US said earlier this month that it is donating small boats to Vietnam as part of a $12.5 million foreign assistance package to support maritime law enforcement and combat illegal fishing. It also delivered five training aircraft to Vietnam in November, the first of 12 jets committed by the US Air Force to improve Vietnam’s pilot training program.

“Our goal is to ensure that Vietnam has what it needs to defend its interests at sea, in the air, on the ground and in cyberspace,” Knapper said.

item.title: Kazakhstan Targets $1 Billion for Venture Fund to Help Startups
item.description:
Kazakhstan’s government unveiled plans to target $1 billion in investment to help regional startups and cutting-edge technologies by launching a venture capital fund of funds next year.

Central Asia’s biggest oil producer has seen the rise of home-grown fintech companies such as US-listed Kaspi.kz, while the authorities have sought to break its dependence on energy revenue. President Kassym-Jomart Tokayev has vowed to reduce the state’s role in the economy and to attract $150 billion in foreign investment by the end of his term in 2029.

Now, the Qazaqstan Venture Group aims to invest in leading global and local venture funds that finance the newest technologies, including artificial intelligence, according to the minister of digital, innovations and aerospace.

“We want startups, including fintech, medtech and AI, in the region to come to Kazakhstan,” Zhaslan Madiyev said in a phone interview. The fund will help the best of them on the way to becoming unicorns, he said.

Read more: Kazakhstan Raised One Notch at Moody’s as Economy Diversifies

Kazakhstan will start attracting money in the first half of 2025, with national development holding Baiterek investing $30 million, Madiyev said. A ministry unit will run the fund, where private investors have given preliminary agreement to provide more than $90 million, he said.

The fund will invest 30% of the pooled money in the central Asian and Caucasus region, with the rest expected to be placed in the US and other international markets, Madiyev said.

Kazakhstan is also in talks with funds from the Middle East and global financial institutions to attract investment for the new initiative, the minister said.

item.title: Nissan Share Rally Burns Traders in Asia’s Most-Shorted Carmaker
item.description:
• It has 22% short interest as percentage of free float
  • Shares have rallied after talks of merger with Honda

It’s been a painful week for traders betting against Nissan Motor Co. , Asia’s most shorted automaker, as its shares soared more than 30% in two days after merger talks with stronger rival Honda Motor Co. emerged.

Short interest in Nissan as a percentage of its free float stood at 22% on Tuesday, a day before the news on the deal sent the stock jumping by the most in at least five decades, according to data from S&P Global. Honda is weighing several options, according to Executive Vice President Shinji Aoyama, which include a merger, a capital tie-up and even the formation of a holding company.

Nissan had been an easy target to sell due to the lack of expectations for any bold measures to improve its earnings, said Tsuguya Onozuka , chief executive officer of Orior Asset Management Inc. The stock will likely move higher if the merger is realized, but short selling could increase again depending on the negotiations, he said.

Short interest in Nissan surged to record highs this year as the shares of the troubled company sank on a huge profit slump, triggered by weak sales in the US and China. Short sellers borrow shares and sell them, hoping to profit by buying them back later at a lower price. Nissan is still down about 25% this year, even after the recent rally.

item.title: Segantii Founder Sadler Pleads Not Guilty to Insider Trading
item.description:
• Trial in Hong Kong court is set to start in May 2026
  • Hedge fund and former trader La Rocca also plead not guilty

Hedge fund firm Segantii Capital Management and its founder Simon Sadler have pleaded not guilty to insider trading, paving the way for the case to go to trial in Hong Kong.

Former trader Daniel La Rocca also pleaded not guilty at a Hong Kong District Court hearing on Thursday. The trial is set to start on May 4, 2026, and last 25 days.

Hong Kong’s securities watchdog in early May criminally charged Segantii, Sadler and La Rocca with insider dealing. The allegations led some banks and investors to reassess their dealings with Segantii, ending its 16-year run as one of the most consistent performers in Asia’s hedge fund industry.

Segantii has returned capital to investors, Bloomberg News reported earlier. Founded in Hong Kong in 2007 with just $26 million, Segantii by the end of March had 151 employees globally including Hong Kong, London, New York and Dubai, according to its website. At the peak in 2021, it oversaw $6.2 billion of assets, ranking it as one of the largest Asia-based hedge funds both by headcount and assets under management.

Hong Kong’s Securities and Futures Commission accused the defendants of trading $1.14 million worth of shares of fashion retailer Esprit Holdings Ltd. before a June 2017 block trade, according to writs of summons dated March 25.

item.title: Britain’s Minimum Wage Pushed Up by Change to Faulty Statistics
item.description:
• Low pay rate is 14 pence higher after the ONS made adjustment
  • Businesses face an extra £368 million in costs due to revision

The UK’s minimum wage has been pushed up by 14 pence (18 cents) an hour as a result of changes to a faulty methodology in statistics that underpin the rate.

A survey by the Office for National Statistics now covers more high earners after being found to under-count that group. The change lifted its estimate of the UK’s median income, which in turn resulted in a higher minimum wage as the UK has a target that the lowest paid staff should earn two thirds of the median salary.

The revision was revealed in a policy paper on Oct. 30, the same day that Chancellor of the Exchequer Rachel Reeves delivered her budget and confirmed a 6.7% hike in the minimum wage to £12.21 per hour, to come into effect next April.

The rise was higher than previously forecast by the Low Pay Commission which said 14 pence was down to the change in the ONS’s methodology — the equivalent of £368 million in extra wage costs for UK employers, according to Bloomberg calculations.

The ONS has been heavily criticized for the quality of its data since it was forced to suspend the Labour Force Survey due to a plunge in response rates. The statistics body has acknowledged it might take years before its employment figures are fully reformed, depriving Bank of England interest-rate setters and the Labour government of key information about the jobs market at a time when both are facing crucial decisions.

Read More: UK Government Forced to Use Own Labor Data Amid Statistics Chaos

Issues around official data are now attracting the attention of private sector companies too. “This year’s increases to National Minimum and Living Wage were significantly above expectations, which underlines the importance of accurate forecasting to give businesses some level certainty and stability in their planning,” said Kate Nicholls , chief executive officer of UKHospitality, a lobby group for British bars, pubs and restaurants.

Many businesses in the sector have said they will struggle to cope with the 37% increase in the minimum wage over four years.

A spokesperson for the ONS said it had improved the treatment of high earners in the survey and improved the quality of estimates.

Opposite Problem

Compounding the uncertainty around the data, some academics fear the minimum wage is now higher than it would be if another problem in the statistics had also been corrected.

A study published in September by the Economic Statistics Centre of Excellence, known as ESCoE, found the ONS survey has been under-representing small, privately-owned firms.

That could lead to the opposite problem of the one caused by under-representation of high earners. Both issues concern the ONS’s Annual Survey of Hours and Earnings, or ASHE.

After adjusting the methodology to better account for business characteristics, ESCoE researchers said the median wage for last year should have been lower than implied by ASHE.

“When the ONS made their adjustment in the last release in November because they were increasing the share of high earners, the median wage went up a little bit,” said John Forth, a professor at Bayes Business School and the lead researcher on the ESCoE report. “If they adjust the weighting methodology more in line with what we’ve done, I’m expecting it will come down more than it went up.”

The researchers also believe the ASHE survey has underestimated the number of UK workers earning the minimum wage by about a fifth. This would mean a greater burden on companies when the minimum wage is lifted — a key consideration of the Low Pay Commission.

“Firms are facing a massive hike in employment costs in 2025 which they had not planned for,” said Jane Gratton, deputy director of public policy at the British Chambers of Commerce . “Our research shows this is already impacting on decisions around hiring and pricing.”

item.title: Scholz’s Far-Right Rival Puts Germany’s EU Exit on the Ballot
item.description:
• AfD co-leader Alice Weidel decries EU’s ‘socialist’ policies
  • No. 2 party in the polls wants border control and cheap energy

Alice Weidel , the long-shot chancellor candidate from the far-right Alternative for Germany, slammed the European Union for destroying the country’s auto industry and proposed winding back the bloc to a free-trade zone.

“What we need to have is free trade among the European countries, but we don’t need all the bureaucracy,” co-leader of the second-strongest party in German polls told Bloomberg TV in Berlin. The EU’s “socialist policy making” has “destroyed the market mechanism in Europe.”

As an example, Weidel — the AfD’s first-ever candidate for chancellor — cited Germany’s automotive sector, which she said had been “ripped off” by an EU ban on the sale of new combustion-engine vehicles by 2035.

“We don’t need all of these bureaucrats who have no clue what they’re doing and destroying our foundation in the European Union,“ Weidel said. “We don’t see that the European Union in its current state is an institution that is working well.”

In its campaign platform for the Feb. 23 snap election, the AfD calls for a German exit from the EU and the euro zone, moves that would mark a major shift in German policy and unwind decades of political and economic integration. The party also calls for a crackdown on undocumented migrants, including expelling hundreds of thousands of people.

The Germany First appeal has resonated with many voters unsettled by the country’s sputtering economy. The anti-immigration party trails only the Christian Democrat-led conservative opposition and is ahead of all ruling parties including Chancellor Olaf Scholz ’s Social Democrats.

Read More: Germany Is Unraveling Just When Europe Needs It Most

In the interview, which was recorded on Wednesday, Weidel pushed back against labeling the party as “far right,” calling it “libertarian” and “conservative.”

Three state chapters in the former communist east are classified as extremist and are under surveillance by Germany’s domestic intelligence service. The party says Islam doesn’t belong in the country, and former leader Alexander Gauland has called the Nazi period a mere blemish on Germany’s long history.

Weidel said that her main priorities, if elected, would be border control, lowering taxes and reviving nuclear power after Germany shut down its last plants in 2023.

The policies though have little chance of being implemented since all other parties have ruled out a coalition with the AfD. Still, the party has had a major influence on the political debate in Germany, notably pushing for a harder line on migration.

The 45-year-old, who has co-chaired the AfD with Tino Chrupalla since 2021, called for reforms of EU treaties that allow member states to veto decisions by the European Commission, the bloc’s executive arm. She also said that countries should be allowed to exit the EU, with the default being a free-trade partnership rather than going through the contortions that accompanied the UK’s Brexit negotiations.

“I highly recommend just a free trade zone because all the bureaucrats — thousands of them — are eating up taxpayers’ money,” she said.


Alice Weidel in Berlin, on Dec. 18. Photographer: Krisztian Bocsi/Bloomberg

Weidel, whose party is an advocate of restoring business and political ties with Russia, welcomed Donald Trump ’s return to the White House. Her party calls for an end of all military aid for Ukraine and has warned against what AfD officials described as further military escalation by the West.

“We have huge hopes with his presidency,” she said. “I really hope that he puts an end to this terrible war in the Ukraine because the European member states are actually not willing or capable of doing something like that.”

Read More: Europe’s Far Right Has Roadmap for a World in Which Trump Wins

The background to the end of the war is the prospect for restarting deliveries of cheap energy from Russia, most notably natural gas. The party, which is skeptical of man-made climate change, is also in favor of burning coal to generate power over an expansion of renewable sources.

Weidel played down concerns that Trump will levee tariffs against German carmakers. Volkswagen AG , BMW AG and Mercedes-Benz Group AG have built factories there to tap demand from American consumers.

Germany’s carmakers “are already in the US,” she said. “And you know why? Because of high energy prices here in Germany.”

item.title: Asia Dealmakers See More IPOs, Convertible Bonds in 2025
item.description:
• Mainland China listings have dipped amid market stabilization
  • Dollar convertible bond sales, India IPO activity seen rising

Investment bankers are pinning hopes on initial public offerings in Asia to grab a bigger slice of equity capital markets volumes in 2025, after such transactions fell to the smallest proportion in eight years as Chinese deals remained muted.

New listings in mainland China have underwhelmed since last August, when the securities regulator tightened local issuance to stabilize its stock market. Similarly, Hong Kong’s IPO market has struggled due to China’s economic woes and Beijing’s regulatory crackdowns on various sectors.

IPOs on both mainland exchanges and in Hong Kong raised just about $27 billion this year through Thursday, on track for the lowest annual volume since 2013, according to data compiled by Bloomberg. Across the whole of Asia Pacific, first-time share sales accounted for just 30% of all ECM volumes, well below the 43% average for the years 2017 to 2023, the data show.

“China volume will continue to grow in 2025,” said James Wang , co-head of equity capital markets for Asia excluding Japan at Goldman Sachs Group Inc. “Investors have come to recognize China risk, but also see valuations being pretty attractive in selective assets.”

China Firms Seeking Second Listings Seen Boosting HK Deal Volume

Meanwhile, follow-on stock offerings and convertible bonds have helped fill the void left by Chinese IPOs. Dollar-denominated convertible and exchangeable bonds by companies in the region have raised a record $29 billion this year, up 40% from the tally in 2023, as companies sought cheap money against the backdrop of high interest rates.

This year saw mammoth deals including those raising $5 billion by Chinese e-commerce company Alibaba Group Holding Ltd. and $3.5 billion by Ping An Insurance (Group) Co.

Convertible-bond issuance is likely to continue in 2025 despite expectations for borrowing costs to fall, Goldman Sachs’ Wang said. “Companies will continue to need to raise capital, and while the market is improving, it will remain volatile.”

A drop in equity issuance by Chinese firms, which historically made up the bulk of the region’s transactions, has resulted in a larger proportion of deals orginating from other countries. IPOs in India have raised a record $19.4 billion this year, with bankers expecting even more proceeds from such listings and follow-on share sales to institutions next year.

In Japan, IPOs have raised $6.3 billion this year, more than each of the previous two years’ annual volumes, thanks to big listings such as Tokyo Metro Co.’s $2 billion-plus share sale. Metal refiner JX Advanced Metals Corp. is among firms slated to go public next year, with a share sale that may raise up to about ¥700 billion ($4.5 billion), Bloomberg News reported .

CATL Is Said to Mull HK Listing to Raise at Least $5 Billion

The magnitude of Chinese equity deals next year hinges on the China Securities Regulatory Commission’s willingness to approve domestic listings. Offshore, the pipeline of Chinese IPOs depends on mainland companies seeking second listings in Hong Kong, a process seen as more straightforward due to these companies’ track records on domestic exchanges.

“A to H is a proven theme,” said Peihao Huang , co-head of Asia-Pacific equity capital markets at JPMorgan Chase & Co., referring to firms seeking a second listing in Hong Kong. Global long-only investors and sovereign-wealth funds have returned to deals this year compared to the last one to two years, she added.

Other Chinese companies in the pipeline for offshore listings include those in artificial intelligence, semiconductors and clean energy — areas that align with the nation’s strategic goals to enhance its technological capabilities, said Christine Xu , a partner at law firm Linklaters LLP, who leads Chinese ECM transactions.

item.title: Europe Car Sales Dip as Automakers Battle Consumer Slowdown
item.description:
• France, Italy lead decline in registrations, lobby group says
  • Demand for fully electric cars improved slightly in November

European car sales sank last month as the region’s biggest automakers grapple with a slowdown in demand that’s triggered an industry crisis.

New-car registrations declined 2% in November from a year earlier to 1.06 million units, led by sharp falls in France and Italy, the European Automobile Manufacturers’ Association said Thursday. Spain was the only major market in the region to see a rise in sales.

Automakers are battling a slowdown in sales in Europe, where consumers remain under pressure from higher living costs. The removal of electric-vehicle subsidies in some countries has further hit demand for low-emission vehicles, which tend to be more expensive than their combustion-engine equivalents.

Europe’s biggest carmakers including Volkswagen AG and Stellantis NV are speeding up their push to cut costs while trying to placate unions. The crisis has also taken its toll on auto suppliers as well as startups such as Swedish battery company Northvolt AB , which filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection in November .

Sales of fully electric cars in Europe edged up 0.9% in November from a year earlier, ACEA said, helped by strong sales in the UK where discounts are boosting demand. They’re still down slightly from January through November. Plug-in hybrid registrations dropped 8.6% last month.

As European buyers struggle to warm to electric cars, registrations of hybrids without a plug are picking up. Sales of the vehicles, which are powered by combustion engines and smaller batteries, rose 16% in November.

Industry Slowdown

The industry’s slowdown is having far-reaching implications. VW is proposing layoffs and unprecedented factory closures, while Stellantis is trying to recover from a disastrous year that culminated in this month’s ousting of former Chief Executive Officer Carlos Tavares . Manufacturers are separately facing billions of euros in fines if they fail to meet stricter European fleet-emissions rules slated to kick in next year.

Suppliers have also announced drastic cost-saving measures in response to the slump in demand. Robert Bosch GmbH , ZF Friedrichshafen AG and Schaeffler AG are among the parts makers that are pursuing thousands of job cuts as the fallout hits the car industry’s supply chain.

Read More: Bosch to Cut 5,500 Jobs as Auto Industry Slump Deepens

Other industries are capitalizing on their problems. Germany’s defense companies are looking to pick up auto workers, with radar maker Hensoldt AG already in talks to hire full teams from two separate auto-parts suppliers.

item.title: UBS Gives a Progress Report on Credit Suisse Clean Up
item.description:
When UBS Group AG agreed to buy Credit Suisse Group AG in March 2023, it was a pivotal moment for European banking. The government-brokered deal was considered historic and its success was instrumental in containing a crisis that had started to spread across global financial markets.

At the center of its success has been Beatriz Martin, head of non-core and legacy operations. Her role is key to this massive restructuring as she sifts through Credit Suisse’s assets and decides which ones to keep and which to wind down. On this week’s episode of In the City , Martin sits down with hosts Francine Lacqua and David Merritt to discuss the process, strategy and progress so far.

“ It has been easier, to be honest, than what we thought at the beginning,” she said. “But I also have to say that [UBS Chief Executive Officer Sergio Ermotti] and the whole executive board has been extremely focused on execution.”

They also discuss the challenges facing the banking industry next year, how European banks can remain competitive against their US peers and what she’s looking forward to next.

Below is a lightly edited transcript of their conversation. Listen to the full episode below, learn more about the podcast here and subscribe on Apple and Spotify to stay on top of new episodes.

Francine Lacqua: And on this week's In The City , we speak with Beatriz Martin, head of non-core and legacy operations at UBS, and also lead on sustainability and impact for the bank. Bea, thank you so much for joining us on In The City . Beatriz Martin: Thank you for having me. Lacqua: So Bea, this is one of your first forays into the media. I've been following you for years and you've had quite an impressive career. How did you actually start in banking?

Martin: I started in banking in the middle of the 90s, uh, really because I love traveling. So, um, I did a, um, exchange program, with the University of Frankfurt. I needed to work while being at that university. That was kind of like the regime there. I did my internship the cooperative bank and then Deutsche Bank. And then I [00:02:00] came back to Madrid and I really wanted to kind of find a job, uh, where I could see the world and, you know, do interesting things.

Martin: And then Deutsche Bank approached me and offered me an international trainee program. And they explained, “you're going to spend time in New York and, you know, in Singapore.” And then I thought, well, yeah, why not? So I took the job and, uh, it's been great. It's been so much fun since then.

Lacqua: Banking has changed quite a lot. What are the main differences you've seen?

Martin: Well, I mean, I started in the trading floor, um, and, and trading and, and then structuring, and, uh, it was a very male dominated environment where, you know, it was very rough, very fast. And, um, I think over time, you know, the tone has changed for the better. I think you see many more women on the trading floor. I think, uh, the banking industry has become much more serving of clients rather than, uh, trading prop, for example. So those are all changes that I think have been very positive for the banking industry over the last years.

David Merritt: We talk on this podcast a lot about the culture around here in the City of London. Diversity being a big [00:03:00] driver of cultural change. Sometimes it feels like two steps forward and one step back. I mean, do you think the progress has been smooth and do you think that we do have a more inclusive culture here in the City?

Martin: I do. I think a lot has happened and changed over the last years. A lot needs to still be done, right? I mean, we all wanted to have a more diverse team and diverse culture to kind of mirror our society, to be fair, and, you know, give opportunities to everybody and try to work on the unconscious bias. Uh, from a very male-dominated, uh, you know, industry, as I said before, but I think banks have made huge efforts in order to improve, uh, the environment and the culture, uh, to be more inclusive, not only to have more diverse, you know, people, but also to give them a voice, uh, representation, have them at the right levels in the right places. But there is no doubt that, you know, much more needs to be done, right? And it is a process, but I think, you know, yeah, we've done really good progress.

Merritt: Obviously the big, the deal of the century in banking was UBS's acquisition of Credit Suisse last year, which you're obviously intimately familiar with. And in the lead up to that, of course, we've talked about that again on this podcast, Francine, as well, the scandals at Credit Suisse, the culture that kept on flaring up into bad headlines for the bank. I mean, has it changed now? The people that you've moved over, has it, has it been absorbed fully now into UBS?

I mean, I think when we, when we took on the team from Create Suisse, and I can say that for me, non-core the majority of my team is actually Create Suisse people, right? Because those were the books they were running, and those were the risks they were in charge of, and I kept quite a lot of them. I mean, frankly, probably north of 90 percent of my team is, is Credit Suisse. I think, um, you have to be very clear in your communication as a leader of what is expected and what is, um, you know, acceptable and not right? And, I kind of was very clear with my team about, you know, what the rules of the road are going to be and what the engagement is going to be.

Personally, I have not seen any anything where, you know, you can say, Oh, the, this culture was really bad. I think there were some individuals I'm sure that, you know, where we're bad. Those you have, um, I wouldn't say in all, you know, banks, but in some of the banks and they don't actually speak for the whole, you know, population and people that are working in the bank. I think there are, you know, selective examples of people that are doing certain things that drive, you know, the bank into trouble. Um, hopefully all of those are, are not with us today, right? I think a lot of them actually left before even the takeover.

But, um, I will say that the culture at Credit Suisse was, was, uh, was one of, uh, maybe more pushing more, you know, trying to do business at all costs that were more focused on revenue than profitability. For example, I think that has been something really important because UBS has been focused on, [00:06:00] you know, return on equity over the last years since really, we repositioned the investment bank into a very capital light business. And so returns have been important for us, much more than revenue. Obviously, we need revenue to make returns, but it's a conflagration of capital footprint costs to operate your business complexity and so on. I think that dimension wasn't that clear to some of the, um, you know, employees at Create Suisse but I will say that it's been, you know, uh, it's been a pleasure to work with them and, you know, I haven't seen any kind of weird.

Merritt: When that deal happened, I mean, you must have thought, I mean, everyone at UBS must have thought, wow, this is a, we've got our work cut out here to integrate these two giants of Swiss banking, but I mean, how did it feel? When that news was breaking, it was such a dramatic moment for the banking industry. We were all gripped by it here.

Lacqua: Bea hasn't slept since.

Martin: It's, you know, I mean, it was a very emotional moment for for Switzerland for sure. You know, the two major banks, you know, one of them failing, being taken by the other bank. They were very stressful days. I was the group treasurer at the time at UBS. We were obviously looking at, you know, what is the deposit and, you know, the liquidity position, the capital position, thinking about, okay, how if they come on, how do we kind of absorb this bank, right?

You know, when, when it all happened on the Sunday and it was clear and then we had a period where we were still kind of together, but separate, I'll say, where we couldn't see all the positions and so on. I think there was quite a lot of uncertainty about what were we taking, right? What was going to be in the books, what was the risk going to look like? And then very rapidly, you know, there was a CEO change at UBS as well. So Ralph left, then, you know, uh, Sergio came back and then, you know, he decided to set up non-core and said, okay, there will be things we don't want to run for the future. We'll put them there. Uh, and then everybody else is going to kind of integrate their own divisions. It has been easier, to be honest, I think, than what we thought at the beginning, in a way. But I also have to say that Sergio, and the whole group, executive board has been extremely focused on execution. And, you know, we, we meet together very often. We talk about, you know, what is working and not. We redirect, you know, our efforts into prioritization of things that are important. So it's been, you know, it's been very intense, and I think it's, it's been really good.

Lacqua: The pace is astonishing. Was it done on purpose that you have to do it well, but actually the quickest is so that you can start flying the plane instead of building it?

Martin: Yes. I think, you know, Sergio was clear that we, we didn't want to linger very long with uncertainty on people, legal entities, setups and so on. So we've really tried to pace it up. Um, and, you know, calculate obviously the risks that you take by doing that. But yeah, I think that has been totally a conscious decision.

Lacqua: Can you indulge us? How does it work? So you're looking through, and I remember at the time there were, you know, saying we don't know when we find or what we find in some of the books in New York or even, you know, some of the trading floors here. I mean, is it all on paper? Are you looking through like hundreds of pages? It's all digitized, right?

Martin: No, it's not digitized and it's not all on paper and, you know, a lot of things you kind of go and try to grab the documents and, you know, do you find them somewhere or another? So there's been quite a lot of effort on that, obviously, particularly on my portfolio, for example, where, you know, you want to sell positions and you need to obviously read all the documents and, you know, have clarity on what the contracts are and the conditions and you know, any obligations, any potential liabilities and so on. So that is, that is something that has taken us obviously quite a lot of time, but no, unfortunately, not everything is digitized.

Merritt: When I was looking at the UBS share price, you know, and obviously there was a lot of anxiety at the time, probably the same that you were describing in the markets. But since then, it's been proven to be a big success.

Martin: I mean, we took a hit, right? I mean, right after the announcement, I think there was, there was quite a lot of, okay, what, what is this going to be? And I think, you know, some of the things that you guys have been mentioning, you know, the pace of integration, I think the results, you know, the businesses coming together. I think the winning back of some of the clients, uh, that, uh, you know, used to be Credit Suisse clients that we won back, the activity of the business and so on.

I think it's been very positive. And, of course, also, I think the pace at which you know we have been reducing the non-core, um, the non-core legacy division, which is very important. I mean, we've returned over 6 billion of capital back to to the group so that the others can grow, of course, and they can be redeployed into client business. So I think, you know, the progress has been really important for the share price to move up.

Lacqua: Was this one of the toughest periods actually in your career? And what did you learn about yourself and the team around you?

Martin: It's been tough because, as I said, I think it's a very emotional moment. I think for many people seeing the bank they've been working for for, I don't know, 25 years going down and then being you know, re-deployed into new teams, taking different positions, at times, more junior positions than what they had before. It's been tough. [00:11:00] Um, what it tells me is that, you know, communication goes a very long way. Being transparent to people, uh, is very important. I think, you know, we are all very used to being resilient, to work in a, you know, stressful environment, uh, to do a lot of things at once and different things. But I think people want to know, you know, where they stand and what the future has in for them. And so giving them some clarity over those parts, I think it's, it's quite, uh, important as a leader.

Merritt: I'm just curious, like going back to what you said at the beginning about what it was like going into a bank when you started, like if this deal had happened 20 years ago with that slightly more macho trading floor, like would all of that transparency, you're talking about, the communication, maybe that wouldn't have happened. Do you think this deal is more likely to succeed because of the culture that you've got at UBS now.

Martin: Yeah, definitely. I think, you know, look, we have been going out of our way to, you know, give people equal opportunities. And I think, you know, everybody's been very clear from the beginning, Sergio, very clearly that we will, you know, take talent from both sides and try to find the right people for the right roles. And, and I think when you look at our numbers, you can see that we've actually quite, I mean, we've integrated quite a lot of people from Create Suisse already. For me, in non-core, as I kind of run down the portfolios and my risk in my books, I need to free up people. I mean, these people, you know, uh, will otherwise go home. But, you know, I think what we have seen is a huge, um, effort and commitment from the group executive board down to find roles for my team as well, right? And this year, we've placed over 280 people in my team already in other parts of the bank and not only the investment bank, by the way, but also other other places.

Lacqua: Bea, when you look at the European, you know, banking landscape, actually, the Swiss banking landscape, do you feel like you can compete with Wall Street?

Martin: I think we can. I think, look, uh, you know, size matters. And over the last years, I think you have seen a disproportionate share of, uh, business going into the US banks, right? And they've been growing quite a lot. But if you look at, you know, European banks and banks in general, I mean, it is a sector that has been outperforming this year. I think 33 percent returns versus, I don't know, 15 percent in the market or so. And if you look at over the last, uh, 4 years, probably like 88 percent or so.

So clearly the banking sector is one that has been, you know, um, benefiting from an environment of high interest rates, uh, you know, low, um, defaults, of course, impairments in the balance sheet and, and really good transactional business. Um, do I think that European banks can compete? I mean, European banks and Europe in general, I think needs to be conscious of the competitiveness position around the world, right?

This is not only in my opinion, it's not only a US issue, right? I mean, I just, I just came back yesterday from Abu Dhabi. It was the Abu Dhabi Financial Week. I mean, when you see what's going on in the Middle East and, you know, the power [00:14:00] of Asia Pacific trying to build the relationships across, you know, Singapore, Hong Kong, all the financial centers are going to be extremely, you know, competitive in trying to gain business that is now much more mobile than was ever before, right?

And this is not only banking. It's tech, it’s biotech, it’s healthcare. It’s a lot of other sectors that are going to be super interesting. You want to attract that talent. You want to attract those, you know, companies into your country to make it stronger and grow.

Merritt: You know, we're taping this on Thursday morning. The European Central Bank have just cut rates again. But within that is a forecast, you know, that the economic outlook in Europe is looking shaky. And then you look, you compare that with America, which seems to be steaming ahead, a lot of kind of inflation coming into the stock market there based on Trump optimism, whether you agree with that or not, but the power, it's powering ahead and the gap seems to be widening. So do you worry about that at all, that Europe's feels like it's stagnating even more, or that [00:15:00] America is steaming ahead over the horizon?

Martin: I think again, I think Europe needs to crack the nut on on trying to see how they can improve on competitiveness, right? And that is true across the board. So all the countries in Europe and together, including the UK, by the way, I think, you know, it's been a tough time for Europe on the growth side. And I think politically, when you see all the tensions that are going on in Europe as well, it's quite a difficult time at the moment. So am I worried? No, I'm not extremely worried because I think you know, there is no other way. They will find a way to, you know, attract the talent and you know, the companies to anchor their businesses in Europe.

I think Europe is, you know, you know, it's a place, you know, with more history. Obviously, we have quite great talent here. I mean, there's a million reasons why people want to live here and want to, you know, have their business here, but we have to make it happen, right?

Merritt: Do worry about politics? I mean, the French, the French situation, the German situation, is this a drag or is it just noise?

[00:16:00] I think people understand that those things will at some point come to a conclusion, right? So I'm not sure that is a huge drag.

Lacqua: What needs to happen to make European banks better? So we had this conversation with Ana Botin, um, on the 10th of December and she was talking about regulation, supervision, the fact that the US will probably power ahead. That Europe, if they don't adapt, will go behind for banks, but also the concern of, you don't really know how you make this economy more competitive. There was a Draghi report that I think was shut down within like an hour of it being sent to capitals around Europe.

And Macron has been saying this, right, and he's just been completely rejected, hasn't he? Like his agenda for kind of making Europe more productive.

bea martin: I think, look, I mean, I think it's interesting. No. So as I said, I was in I was in the Abu Dhabi Finance Week this week. It's incredible when you see what's going on there. But they are building on a new platform, right? I mean, when I say Europe has more history. What I mean is we actually have, whether it's [00:17:00] banks or, you know, manufacturing industries that are sitting on a legacy infrastructure, right? And so making those changes just requires a huge capital expenditure that if you are in Abu Dhabi, or if you are in, you know, other places, simply you don't drag that legacy, right?

So I think the transformation of the European economy just requires probably an investment that over the last years has been going elsewhere. Right? Even from Europe, right/. So, you know, I think again, the, the, the key is to figure out how can you become more competitive? How can you attract those investments into your country to make that transformation happen? But again, I think the foundation is incredible in Europe, to be honest.

Lacqua: Bea, there's also a massive shift when, when it comes to markets, into private markets. I mean, this is incredible, like, like, the amount by which I think it's, it's grown is also expected, uh, certainly in credit to double, uh, in the next couple of years. What does it mean [00:18:00] for, for European banks?

Martin: Well, I think banks, uh, you know, play a very important role in the distribution of some of those products. Uh, not everybody has access to, you know, private markets, private equity, private credit, uh, funds. UBS, for example, is one of the biggest players there. If you think about, you know, the amount of, those assets that we distribute a year through our wealth management network. We have a huge, you know, aggregation power, right? So what does that mean? Is that, you know, we are going to see a diaspora or a dispersion of the credit markets going into, you know, investors that are willing to do direct investments that you didn't see before, right? And whether they are funds or they are actually investors, because it is actually very interesting to see that investors are now looking for direct investment opportunities or rather than, you know, investing into funds. Right? And so I think over time, what you're going to see is is that, you know, more and more clients will [00:19:00] want to have access to those markets. Now, it's also true, by the way, that, you know, there's still, if you look at private equity, for example, you still see quite a lot of dry powder being ready.

You see far less monetization of those, you know, assets than what you saw years ago. And, and I think, you know, you see a huge movement into the secondary market, i.e. providing liquidity to all those deals that are not actually exiting into IPO or else. And now, you know, all the continuation funds, all the secondary funds are kind of taking shape in order to facilitate that flow, right, which is, it's quite interesting to see.

Merritt: And so the public markets then are destined to continue to shrink, do you think? I mean, the stock markets across Europe?

Martin: No, I think, look, public markets are always gonna be, you know, uh, a very important source of investment, particularly for those clients who like liquidity, right? Because at the end of the day, what you have in all these private investments is that, you know, you have either lockout[00:20:00] periods, or you know, your liquidity is only every quarter and so on. So I think you as an investor, you always need a healthy mix of both. I don't think, you know, public markets are there now.

Lacqua: We're talking about secondary markets. What does that mean for opportunities to, you know, for banks that are already quite invested in the private markets? What, what happens to them next or what do they need to focus on next?

Martin: I think it's a great opportunity for banks, but also for investors to think about, you know, takeout techniques or, you know, strategies of some of those funds, that need to create liquidity to either, you know, pay back to investors or continue, you know, kind of their, their investment strategy. And, I think at the right price, that is definitely, you know, a good place to get into.

Lacqua: Who's your biggest competition? I mean, when you look at the private markets growing, do you see a quite important competitor in this space?

Martin: I think what we have seen is everybody, um, so [00:21:00] funds trying to approach clients directly, right? So you see the Apollos, the Carlysles, the Ares, you know, all these guys. If, if you look at the, their growth plans, they're all focusing on wealth management, right? So it's quite interesting to see how, you know, I think we need to make sure that we are still, you know, providing different tailor-made solutions to our clients, access, you know, and so on that will differentiate us from, you know, from, uh, from the providers that we have in the back.

Lacqua: You've been in this job since UBS took over Credit Suisse. It has an expiry date. Once you get rid of all the stuff that you need to get rid of?

Merritt: How long is left?

Martin: Um, well, the official expiry date has always been 2026. Sergio always said that, you know, post-2026, he wants to draw the line and not talk about integration anymore. And I guess I'm part of, you know, that integration. So I've always been thinking that, you know, the end of ‘26 is probably the end of, uh, my job as non-core. [00:22:00] And of course, when I put a deadline, I always try to, you know, kind of, uh, under promise and over deliver. So, I mean, we've made huge progress already with the portfolio.

If you look at, you know, the reductions that we've done, we're probably done about 70 percent or so of the portfolio. There is still a lot of work to do, by the way. So, you know, we are in charge of the commissioning, all the infrastructure, the licensing, all the legal entities, and that just takes time. So hopefully, you know, we are going to see a huge progress over the course of ‘25, which I mean, it's a very important year for the bank. It's a very important year for non-core. I think that that's what you can expect. Lacqua: And what will you do next? Martin: What will I do next? Well, I mean, as you know, um, I am the chief executive for the UK. Um, I'm running the EMEA region, so, uh, Europe, Middle East, and Africa. And I'm also the group executive board lead for sustainability. So I actually have many hats and I will be busy with that. I've been spending lately as you know, as the non core [00:23:00] efforts are, you know, giving fruits. Uh, and the teams have been very much, you know, focused on executing that. I've been spending much more time with clients than what I did at the beginning. And you know, clients is something I love. I've done for many years, uh, before and, uh, and I think that is a place where I can add a lot of value.

I mean, despite all the conversations that we've been having Europe is not dead. There is a huge amount of things to do. And it's a very exciting, exciting place to be where we still see growth, to be honest. So I definitely will be, you know, spending more time on all those things.

Lacqua: Bea Martin, thank you so much for joining us.

Martin: Thank you for having me.

item.title: Glencore Boosts Middle East Oil Buying for Singapore Bukom Plant
item.description:
• Merchant acquires Al-Shaheen, Upper Zakum grades, traders say
  • Company bought refining complex from Shell earlier this year

Glencore Plc has emerged as an active buyer of spot oil cargoes from the Middle East this month, as the merchant starts procuring crude for the Bukom refinery in Singapore, according to traders.

The company purchased three cargoes of Qatar’s Al-Shaheen grade and at least one of Abu Dhabi’s Upper Zakum, the traders said, asking not to be identified as they’re not authorized to speak publicly. The shipments will load in February, they added, and can arrive in Asia the same month, or in March.

Glencore has been gearing up for a bigger role in the physical oil market after acquiring the Bukom refining and chemicals complex from Shell Plc earlier this year with Indonesia’s PT Chandra Asri Pacific . The refinery includes a 237,000 barrel-a-day crude distillate unit.

A spokesperson for Glencore declined to comment on the matter.

In recent years, cash-rich energy traders have been eager to pick up physical assets such as aging refineries and distribution networks as Big Oil speeds up its divestment from fossil-fuel plays due to shareholder pressure. The assets provide merchants with greater exposure to physical and paper markets.

Glencore has recently hired Aditya Ravavarapu from BP Plc to lead its Asian oil trading team, Bloomberg reported last week. Ravavarapu had traded physical crude in the region and worked with BP’s assets in Australia, which were refineries before they were shuttered.

The traders said Glencore is expected to play a bigger role in physical markets from around February or March next year as it takes over the job of procuring oil for the Bukom complex, which predominantly runs on sour crude. The recently purchased cargoes were 500,000 barrels each, they added. They may still be traded or so-called optimized, depending on market conditions.

The refinery previously processed grades that included Saudi Arabian crude, which was supplied by Shell as part of a long-term contract with state-owned producer Aramco .

item.title: Yen’s Outlook Worsens as BOJ Skips Rate Hike, Strategists Say
item.description:
• If Ueda is noncommittal, yen has more downside: RBC Capital
  • Dollar-yen is still dominated by Fed rate outlook, SEB says

The yen ’s outlook is deteriorating after the Bank of Japan skipped a potential rate hike, while the central bank’s economic statement can be seen as somewhat dovish, according to strategists.

The cautious BOJ , compared with the Fed’s hawkish tilt Wednesday, may give further impetus for carry traders to bet on future yen weakness, Saxo Markets says. Attention will now shift to Governor Kazuo Ueda’s press conference later Thursday, but there’s a limit to how hawkish he can be, according to Nomura Securities.

Here is a selection of comments from strategists:

RBC Capital Markets ( Alvin T. Tan , head of Asia FX strategy in Singapore)

Commonwealth Bank of Australia ( Carol Kong , strategist in Sydney)

Saxo Markets ( Charu Chanana , chief investment strategist in Singapore)

Nomura Securities ( Yujiro Goto , head of FX strategy in Tokyo)

Skandinaviska Enskilda Banken ( Eugenia Victorino , head of Asia strategy in Singapore)

Credit Agricole CIB ( David Forrester , senior strategist in Singapore)

Mitsubishi UFJ Morgan Stanley Securities ( Kohei Onishi , senior investment strategist in Tokyo)
